coredata.dc:title: Near-infrared threonine-quercetin carbon dots for bioimaging and dual protection against liver and brain injury via oral and intravenous administration

coredata.dc:description: Oxidative stress, resulting from the overproduction of reactive oxygen species (ROS), represents a significant risk factor for the development of pathological conditions, including liver injury and brain injury (characterized by neurological damage). There is a compelling requirement to investigate more convenient and efficacious drug delivery strategies for the treatment of hepatic and neurological injuries. In this study, near-infrared threonine-quercetin carbon dots (TQ CDs) with antioxidant activity were synthesized. The bioimaging results demonstrated that the TQ CDs were capable of aggregating in both the liver and the brain via both intravenous and gavage administration. Furthermore, we present a straightforward methodology for investigating the relationship between the concentration of intravenous administration and the concentration of gavage administration. Our findings indicate that the intravenous bioimaging effect and biological effect of intravenous administration could be achieved by increasing the concentration of the TQ CDs administered via gavage. Also, the biological effects including protection of the liver and brain were corroborated through in vivo liver injury and neurological injury models. Overall, this study opens up an explorable field of nanoparticle-based oral drug delivery strategies for bioimaging, organ protection, and treating liver and brain diseases.

head.ce:title: Near-infrared threonine-quercetin carbon dots for bioimaging and dual protection against liver and brain injury via oral and intravenous administration

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Oxidative stress, resulting from the overproduction of reactive oxygen species (ROS), represents a significant risk factor for the development of pathological conditions, including liver injury and brain injury (characterized by neurological damage). There is a compelling requirement to investigate more convenient and efficacious drug delivery strategies for the treatment of hepatic and neurological injuries. In this study, near-infrared threonine-quercetin carbon dots (TQ CDs) with antioxidant activity were synthesized. The bioimaging results demonstrated that the TQ CDs were capable of aggregating in both the liver and the brain via both intravenous and gavage administration. Furthermore, we present a straightforward methodology for investigating the relationship between the concentration of intravenous administration and the concentration of gavage administration. Our findings indicate that the intravenous bioimaging effect and biological effect of intravenous administration could be achieved by increasing the concentration of the TQ CDs administered via gavage. Also, the biological effects including protection of the liver and brain were corroborated through in vivo liver injury and neurological injury models. Overall, this study opens up an explorable field of nanoparticle-based oral drug delivery strategies for bioimaging, organ protection, and treating liver and brain diseases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) are a series of intermediates in the reduction process of molecular oxygen, mainly including superoxide radical, hydrogen peroxide, singlet oxygen and hydroxyl radical [1]. ROS with inherent biochemical properties participate in various biological phenomena and are essential for the development of living organisms [2]. Generally, these chemical species are involved in cell signaling and play a significant role in the regulation of various physiological functions in organisms [3,4]. Nevertheless, the overproduction of ROS is associated with oxidative stress, which represents a risk factor for the development of organ damage and neurodegenerative diseases [5].

Specifically, the detrimental effects of heavy metal exposure are strongly correlated with oxidative stress [6]. Heavy metals, such as cadmium (Cd), are common pollutants that induce oxidative stress and produce ROS, which have deleterious effects on various body systems, including the liver [7]. Therefore, the issue of heavy metal-induced cellular and organismal toxicity may be resolved by the inhibition of oxidative stress [8]. In addition, the progression of neurodegenerative diseases such as Parkinson's disease (PD) is strongly associated with redox dysfunctions, and a substantial body of research has demonstrated that individuals with Parkinson's disease frequently exhibit markedly elevated levels of oxidative stress [9–12]. Similarly, it may be feasible to mitigate the brain damage caused by the progressive loss of dopaminergic neurons in individuals with PD by suppressing levels of oxidative stress [13].

Overall, the protection of organs, including the liver and brain, may be achieved by the suppression of oxidative stress. Previous studies demonstrated that selenium and flavonoids exhibited remarkable protection against cadmium-induced liver injury [14,15]. Furthermore, the beneficial palliative and therapeutic effects of piceid and an analog of vitamin E in the treatment of PD have been demonstrated according to previous studies, similarly [16,17]. Indeed, all of these contributors are occupied with antioxidant activity. However, the small molecules with antioxidant effects exhibit poor water solubility, blood–brain barrier (BBB) permeability and ineffective accumulation in target organs. Fortunately, the emergence of nanomaterials is gradually addressing these challenges on an ongoing basis. Studies have shown that nanomaterials exhibit a superior accumulation effect in the liver and are more prone to penetrate the BBB [18–20].

Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a promising zero-dimensional nanomaterial with a range of superior properties. These characteristics include fluorescent properties, electronic and physicochemical attributes, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility [21,22]. Consequently, CDs were extensively employed in bioimaging, electrocatalysis, the treatment of disease, and other related fields [23–25]. Previous studies have addressed the antioxidant properties of carbon dots and their potential applications. For example, the in vitro antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects of genistein CDs have been demonstrated [26], and the use of positively charged CDs with antioxidant activities has been evidenced to expedite the healing of infected wounds [27]. Additionally, the utilization of lignin-based crude carbon dots as an efficacious antioxidant for natural rubber is also a promising avenue of research [28]. Furthermore, N-acetylcysteine-derived CDs with the ability to scavenge free radicals have been used in the study of intervertebral disc degeneration [29]. It is evident that research into CDs with antioxidant properties has been conducted across a range of disciplines. Nevertheless, the studies on the protective effects against liver and brain injury remain insufficient. Specifically, there is a paucity of research investigating the protective effects of CDs on liver damage induced by heavy metals and neurological injury associated with PD. Given the strong association between these injuries and oxidative stress as well as the advantages of the nanomaterials mentioned above, there is an urgent need to investigate new antioxidant CDs with superior and promising properties to inhibit this oxidative stress. Consequently, it is of paramount significance that these CDs are able to accumulate efficiently in the liver and easily cross the BBB. Additionally, in the field of bioimaging and therapeutic models of disease, CDs are employed in the form of injections. However, there has been a paucity of research into the oral delivery of CDs, particularly in the context of models of brain diseases such as PD. This study addresses the aforementioned challenges and successfully expands the field of research on antioxidant CDs.

In this study, the synthesis of TQ CDs was conducted using threonine and a minimal quantity of quercetin by the hydrothermal method, and the properties of TQ CDs were subjected to a detailed characterization. Then, the antioxidant capacity of TQ CDs was evaluated, including the assessment of their ability to scavenge ROS and free radicals. It is worth noting that TQ CDs are near-infrared CDs and are capable of penetrating deeper into tissue than carbon dots with emission in visible regions, which allows for more effective bioimaging. Therefore, the traditional intravenous injection method and the convenient gavage method were employed for the imaging of mice, resulting in the detection of significant fluorescent signals in the liver and brain. It is noteworthy that TQ CDs can be administered by gavage into both the brain and the liver, representing an exciting discovery in this field. Here, taking advantage of the intrinsic fluorescent properties and imaging capabilities of TQ CDs, this study presented a simple and convenient approach to calculate effective gavage concentrations. The bioimaging capabilities and biological effects of TQ CDs administered via gavage are comparable to those achieved by intravenous injection. Finally, in consideration of the aggregation potential of antioxidant TQ CDs in the liver and brain, two murine models of cadmium-induced liver injury and MPTP-induced Parkinson's disease were established, respectively. And the protective effects of TQ CDs on the liver and brain of mice were also evaluated by the administrations of gavage and intravenous injection (Scheme 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of TQ CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The precursor compounds for the synthesis of CDs were selected to be the essential amino acid threonine as the main component, which has a favorable safety profile, and a small quantity of quercetin was also included. Notably, both threonine and quercetin have been demonstrated to possess antioxidant activity and the capacity to penetrate the BBB [30–33]. The retention effect of CDs ensures the preservation of the functional groups of the precursor compounds, thereby allowing the biological effects of the precursor compounds to be retained [34,35]. Meanwhile, the conversion of quercetin to water-soluble CDs may enhance its antioxidant effectiveness by increasing its solubility. In addition, it has been demonstrated that threonine exerts a protective effect against cadmium-induced liver injury [36], and quercetin has been shown to have beneficial effects on Parkinson's disease [37]. Taking into account the above factors, the two compounds were combined, resulting in the successful synthesis of TQ CDs, which may simultaneously address oxidative stress-related damage in the liver and brain.

Subsequently, the synthesized carbon dots were characterized. the optical properties of TQ CDs were investigated through the analysis of their ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption and fluorescence spectra. Firstly, the inherent fluorescent properties of TQ CDs were evaluated. The visible fluorescence spectrum of TQ CDs and an image of TQ CDs under UV light excitation with a wavelength of 365 nm were presented in Fig. S1. It can be observed that in the excitation wavelength range from 360 nm to 460 nm, the emission peaks of the TQ CDs exhibit a gradual red shift as the excitation wavelength increases. At an excitation wavelength of 380 nm, the TQ CDs exhibited the greatest fluorescence intensity. Notably, TQ CDs belong to the near-infrared (NIR) carbon dots and also exhibit distinct fluorescence emission peaks in the near-infrared region (>700 nm) [38,39]. The low degree of photon absorption, scattering, and autofluorescence from tissues enables the realization of deep tissue penetration in this spectral range [40]. The NIR fluorescence spectrum of TQ CDs is presented in Fig. 1 A. In the excitation wavelength range from 600 nm to 660 nm, no notable alteration in the emission peak position was observed. The difference is that in the excitation wavelength range from 680 nm to 760 nm, the emission peaks are red-shifted with increasing excitation wavelength. Additionally, the imaging of different concentrations of TQ CDs was conducted, and the average fluorescence intensity was calculated by CleVue software (supplied with In-vivo Optical Imager) (Fig. 1B). This revealed a linear relationship between the concentration of TQ CDs and the fluorescence intensity. Fig. 1C exhibits the multi-exponential fluorescence decay behavior. The average fluorescence lifetime of TQ CDs was calculated to be about 3.42 ns at an excitation wavelength of 740 nm. In general, TQ CDs exhibit superior fluorescent properties. Fig. 1D depicts the ultraviolet absorption spectra of threonine, quercetin and TQ CDs. It is notable that the distinctive absorption peak at 197 nm of the TQ CDs is analogous to that of threonine. Similarly, the obvious absorption peak of TQ CDs at 272 nm is analogous to that of quercetin. In addition, two weak signals from TQ CDs were observed at 304 nm and 373 nm, where quercetin showed similar characteristics. The aforementioned findings collectively indicate that TQ CDs retain the structural and chemical characteristics of threonine and quercetin.

In order to gain insight into the structure and composition of TQ CDs, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were employed as analytical techniques. The FTIR results demonstrated the presence of TQ CDs, with the absorption peaks observed at 3420, 2980, 1620, 1496 and 1412 cm−1 (Fig. 1E). The FTIR results show that the distinctive absorption peak of TQ CDs at 3420 cm−1 was due to the stretching vibration of –NH2/–OH. The absorption peak at 2980 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching of C-H bonds in aromatic or aliphatic molecules. The peaks observed at 1620 cm−1 and 1412 cm−1 are respectively attributed to the antisymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the carboxyl group (COO–). Additionally, the aromatic benzene ring may exhibit C=C stretching, which could result in the appearance of an absorption peak at 1496 cm−1. The XPS results (Fig. 1F) indicated that TQ CDs were composed of three elements: C, N, and O, and the elemental composition of the TQ CDs was determined to be C 66.94 %, N 6.94 %, and O 26.12 %, as indicated by the results of the analysis. The C1s, N1s, and O1s peaks exhibited distinct positions, with approximate values of 284.8 eV, 401 eV, and 532 eV, respectively. Specifically, the high-resolution XPS analysis of C1s (Fig. 1G) revealed three distinct peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.3 eV, and 288 eV, which correspond to the following functional groups: C=C/C-C, C-O, and C=O, respectively. Also, two peaks at 399.3 eV and 401.2 eV, were respectively attributed to amine N and graphitic N according to the N1s spectrum (Fig. 1H). The two obvious peaks observed at 530.8 eV and 532 eV can be attributed to the presence of C-O and C=O, respectively (Fig. 1I). These results were found to be largely consistent with those obtained by FTIR results. Then, the size and morphology of TQ CDs were studied by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). The TEM images show that the TQ CDs were distributed in a well-dispersed manner, with a diameter in the range of 2–10 nm. A statistical analysis of 100 particles conducted using the ImageJ software program revealed that the majority of the TQ CDs were in the size range of approximately 6.06 nm (Fig. 1J). As illustrated in Fig. 1K, the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image revealed the presence of distinct lattice streaks within the TQ CDs, with an average spacing of 0.21 nm. As illustrated in Fig. 1L, the AFM image reveals that the particle height of TQ CDs is approximately 3.6 nm. Concurrently, the Zeta potential of TQ CDs was −18.4 mV in ultrapure water and −13.3 mV in PBS, suggesting that TQ CDs exhibit excellent stability (Fig. S2). In general, TQ CDs are basically consistent with the morphological characteristics of carbon dots.

A series of in vitro and in vivo experiments were conducted to assess the biosafety of TQ CDs. The safety of the materials was evaluated through cytotoxicity and hemolysis tests (Fig. S3A-F). The biocompatibility of TQ CDs has been demonstrated to be excellent, with no significant hemolysis observed. Furthermore, the biosafety of TQ CDs was demonstrated through the evaluation of body weight, liver and kidney function, and H&E staining of organs in mice (Fig. S3G-J). In comparison to the control group, the mice in the TQ CDs group exhibited no significant alterations in body weight. Additionally, the livers and kidneys of the mice exhibited normal functionality, with no discernible damage to the organ tissues.

The antioxidant capacity of TQ CDs was evaluated. The well-known antioxidant ascorbic acid was employed in a comparative analysis of antioxidant capacity. Overall, the scavenging of free radicals by TQ CDs is concentration-dependent, with higher concentrations resulting in greater scavenging ability. Fig. 2 A demonstrates the relationship between the concentration of TQ CDs and the scavenging rate of ABTS+·. The variation in UV absorption of ABTS induced by the addition of different concentrations of TQ CDs is shown in Fig. 1B. Also, Fig. S4A exhibits the relationship between time and the scavenging rate of ABTS+·. Similarly, Fig. 2C demonstrates the scavenging capacity of TQ CDs for DPPH·, and Fig. 2D shows the variation of DPPH UV absorption with the addition of TQ CDs. Fig. 2E exhibits the scavenging capacity of TQ CDs for ·O2 -. Fig. 2F shows the alteration in the absorbance of pyrogallol undergoing autoxidation, and the capacity of the TQ CDs to impede the autoxidation of pyrogallol. Fig. S4B exhibits the relationship between time and the scavenging rate of ·O2 -. The scavenging capacity of ·OH was also evaluated at varying concentrations of TQ CDs (Fig. 2G-H). The scavenging of ABTS+·, DPPH· and ·OH by 100 μg/mL of TQ CDs was found to be 96.3 %, 79.7 %, and 94.7 %, respectively. The scavenging of ·O2 - was observed at a concentration of 400 μg/mL TQ CDs, with a scavenging efficiency of 88.6 %. Furthermore, a comparative analysis was conducted to evaluate the free radical scavenging abilities of TQ CDs and precursor compounds (Fig. 2I). The results indicate that TQ CDs exhibit a greater antioxidant capacity than their precursor compounds. Additionally, the scavenging capacity of TQ CDs for ROS was evaluated using RAW 264.7 cells. In the LPS-induced positive control group, a distinct green fluorescence was observed, indicative of the presence of ROS. However, the fluorescence signal was found to be significantly attenuated by the addition of TQ CDs (Fig. 2J). Fig. 2K illustrates the statistical analysis of the mean fluorescence intensity of the fluorescence images. Overall, TQ CDs demonstrated the capacity to retain and enhance the antioxidant potential of the precursor compounds, as well as the ability to scavenge ROS.

The photobleaching resistance of TQ CDs was systematically evaluated prior to their application in bioimaging studies. Fig. S5 demonstrates the changes in the fluorescence spectra of TQ CDs after UV lamp irradiation and the changes in fluorescence intensity under the In-vivo Optical Imager. The results demonstrate that TQ CDs exhibit remarkable photobleaching resistance and show promising potential for bioimaging applications. Fig. S6A-C shows the imaging of TQ CDs on RAW 264.7 cells, HT22 cells, and HUVEC cells. The obvious red fluorescence of the TQ CDs could be observed. Given the potential of TQ CDs for cellular imaging, an evaluation was undertaken to assess the in vivo imaging capability of TQ CDs in mice and the ex vivo imaging ability on organs. C57BL/6j mice were selected for the study, and the traditional intravenous method was employed for bioimaging. Fig. S6D shows the imaging of mice at varying time points following the administration of a specific concentration of TQ CDs. A distinct fluorescent signal was observed in the hepatic region at 6 h, which subsequently diminished at 16 h. This suggests that TQ CDs can be imaged in vivo. However, further investigation is required to ascertain their capacity to aggregate at the liver site. Therefore, the organs of the mice were extracted and observed at 8 h. In comparison with other organs, the liver still exhibited a distinctive fluorescent signal (Fig. S6E). Furthermore, Fig. S6F shows the ex vivo imaging of the liver at various time points and the statistics of the mean fluorescence intensity using Clevue software. The liver exhibited a discernible fluorescent signal, thereby indicating that TQ CDs were capable of accumulating in the liver. The collective findings indicated that TQ CDs possess remarkable imaging capabilities and are predominantly prone to aggregation in the liver.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative imaging of TQ CDs in the liver
ce:section.ce:para: In previous studies, the application of carbon dots was predominantly in the form of injections [41–43]. Nevertheless, intravenous injections remain a cumbersome and poorly accepted method for bioimaging and disease treatment, particularly among specialized demographics such as the elderly and children. Previous studies demonstrated that mice develop hepatic and intestinal circulation following the intravenous injection of CDs into the tail vein, which suggests the potential for CDs to be absorbed through oral administration [44,45]. Oral administration is a convenient and rapid method of administration, while further investigation is required to ascertain the efficacy of CDs for oral administration. Previous studies on oral CDs have demonstrated that the induction of oxidative stress by oral malting barley CDs is a causal factor in the development of insulin resistance in mice [46]. Therefore, the efficacy of CDs when administered orally has been identified and evaluated, and oral administration of CDs may be feasible for both bioimaging and disease treatment. Furthermore, from the perspective of the precursor compounds of TQ CDs, L-threonine is frequently employed as an additive in animal feed and food products [47], and oral administration of quercetin has also demonstrated efficacy [48,49]. Consequently, as the TQ CDs retain the functional group of the precursor compound, they may retain or even increase their oral absorption capacity. The preceding analysis was employed as a foundation for the assessment of the bioimaging capabilities of TQ CDs by intravenous injection and gavage, with the objective of further investigating the feasibility of gavage administration of TQ CDs.

Fig. 3 A-B illustrates the bioimaging of TQ CDs at different time points under the two modes of administration. Fluorescence signals emanating from the liver were observed by both methods. Nevertheless, the fluorescence imaging of gavage administration was not as pronounced as that of intravenous administration. This discrepancy can be attributed to differences in bioavailability. Following intravenous injection of TQ CDs, liver tissues were collected at predetermined time points for ex vivo imaging analysis, and the results showed high correlation with in vivo imaging observations (Fig. 3A). Notably, the results revealed that the hepatic accumulation of TQ CDs gradually diminished and eventually disappeared within about 16 h post-injection (Fig. S7). Also, the imaging of the extracted organs at 8 h also demonstrated the aggregation of TQ CDs in the liver (Fig. 3D-E). Similarly, the observed differences in fluorescence intensity can be attributed to incomplete oral absorption. Consequently, we propose a simple and interesting method for calculating the requisite concentration of gavage to achieve a similar effect to that of intravenous administration. The underlying principle is based on the existence of a linear relationship between the concentration of TQ CDs and the observed fluorescence (Fig. 1B). Fig. 3C depicts the liver images of mice treated with varying concentrations of TQ CDs at 8 h, with the mean fluorescence intensity of the livers subsequently quantified. The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity in the liver and the administered concentration of TQ CDs was still maintained. (Fig. 3F). It is noteworthy that the identical fluorescence intensity indicates that the concentration of accumulated TQ CDs in the liver is approximately equivalent. Therefore, the ratio of the slopes of the two straight lines multiplied by the intravenous concentration represents the required gavage concentration to achieve a similar effect to that given intravenously. In other words, for the liver, the administration of 4.45 times the concentration of TQ CDs via gavage can achieve results comparable to those of an intravenous injection (Fig. 3F). Finally, liver homogenates were prepared and emission peaks were also detected in the homogenate supernatant (Fig. 3G-H). The fluorescence peaks of CDs exhibited no discernible alteration in shape or position, indicating that CDs did not undergo substantial structural modifications during their absorption into the bloodstream via the intestine. This finding is further supported by the fluorescence stability of CDs under varying pH conditions (Fig. S8). The gavage group (CDs (i.g.)) exhibited a more pronounced fluorescence intensity than the intravenous group (CDs (i.v.)) (Fig. 3H). This may be attributed to the fact that the homogenate in the gavage-administered group reached an optimal dilution ratio. Nevertheless, it is irrefutable that TQ CDs administered by gavage do accumulate in the liver. In conclusion, both modes of administration demonstrate excellent imaging and hepatic aggregation capabilities, and it is feasible to achieve intravenous efficacy by increasing the dosage of TQ CDs administered via gavage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protective effect of TQ CDs against liver injury
ce:section.ce:para: Given the antioxidant capacity of TQ CDs and their demonstrated ability to aggregate in the liver, the protective capacity of TQ CDs against liver injury was evaluated in vivo. The 8-week-old male mice were used to construct the model of cadmium-induced liver injury (Fig. 4 A). Fig. 4E shows the fluctuations in body weight of the mice over a seven-day period, and the reduction in body weight was observed in the mice in the liver injury group. Fig. 4B illustrates the liver index (calculated as the liver weight divided by the body weight of the mice) for each experimental group. The liver index of the mice in the liver injury group exhibited a significant elevation. Additionally, the liver function of the mice was also assessed. The treatment group exhibited a statistically significant reduction in the elevation of AST and ALT levels induced by cadmium (Fig. 4C-D). Moreover, the results of the H&E staining of the liver revealed the presence of lymphocyte infiltration between hepatocytes and hepatocyte apoptosis in the mice within the liver injury group. In contrast, no significant differences were observed between the treatment group and the PBS group. These results demonstrated that TQ CDs possess a protective effect against liver injury induced by cadmium (Fig. 4F). However, underlying mechanisms are not the focus of this study. The possible mechanisms of effective protection from TQ CDs were discussed according to the previous study. Threonine was demonstrated to have a significant inhibitory effect on the inflammatory responses induced by cadmium, including the inhibition of tumor necrosis factor-α and interleukin-6 expression, as well as the inhibition of elevated pro-apoptotic proteins. Furthermore, it restored the level of anti-apoptotic protein [36]. Additionally, it has been demonstrated that quercetin can reduce endoplasmic reticulum stress in liver injury induced by cadmium [50]. TQ CDs retain the properties of threonine and quercetin and thus may exert biological effects through the aforementioned mechanisms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative imaging of TQ CDs in the brain
ce:section.ce:para: In the section on TQ CDs synthesis, the potential for threonine and quercetin to cross the BBB was discussed. Nevertheless, the capacity of TQ CDs to traverse the BBB remains to be elucidated.

The imaging of zebrafish revealed that those treated with TQ CD exhibited bright fluorescent signals in the brain at both 488 nm and 638 nm excitation wavelengths (Fig. 5 A). This suggests that TQ CDs are capable of traversing the BBB. To further ascertain the capacity of TQ CDs to traverse the BBB, in vivo imaging was conducted in nude mice and ex vivo imaging of the brain was performed. Similarly, the capacity of TQ CDs to image the brain in vivo was assessed through both intravenous and gavage methods. Fig. 5B shows the imaging of nude mice at different time points following the administration of a specific concentration of TQ CDs. Significant alterations in the fluorescent signals were observed in the brain region of nude mice. The fluorescence signal was observed to be most intense at 4 h for the intravenous injection group and at 6 h for the gavage group. This suggests that intravenous injection of TQ CDs may result in faster brain penetration. It was observed that the nude mice that were administered by gavage exhibited a more pronounced fluorescent signal than those that were administered intravenously. This is due to the fact that the backs of the nude mice in the intravenous administration group exhibited a fluorescent signal from the outset. Actually, the fluorescence image demonstrates the fluorescence under contrasting conditions. When the fluorescence on the posterior aspect is bright, the fluorescence on the brain is less pronounced. A comparison of the imaging of brains extracted at different times revealed a consistency with the in vivo imaging results (Fig. 5C). Concurrently, quantification of cerebral fluorescence intensity demonstrated that TQ CDs showed minimal brain accumulation potential after 8 h post-injection, with near-background signal levels observed (Fig. S9). A similar approach was used to image the brain, with different concentrations of TQ CDs (Fig. 5D). This revealed a linear relationship between brain fluorescence and the concentration of the administered TQ CDs (Fig. 5E). For the brain, the administration of 3.896 times the concentration of TQ CDs via gavage can achieve results comparable to those of an intravenous injection. It is noteworthy that the observed fluorescence in brain tissue extracted after 4 h was not the strongest, due to the longer time required for TQ CDs administered by gavage to reach the brain. Consequently, the administration of TQ CDs via gavage resulted in a greater tendency for aggregation in the brain than in the liver, in comparison to intravenous administration. A preliminary study was conducted to investigate the potential mechanism of TQ CDs crossing the BBB. The in vivo imaging results demonstrated that the simultaneous intravenous administration of threonine and TQ CDs resulted in a reduction of fluorescent signals in the brain when compared with the intravenous administration of TQ CDs alone (Fig. 5F). Similarly, the administration of threonine resulted in a notable reduction of fluorescent signals in the brain following gavage administration of TQ CDs (Fig. 5G). The ex vivo imaging results were found to be generally concordant with the in vivo imaging results (Fig. S10). Furthermore, given that amino acids traverse the BBB via amino acid transporters and the retention effect of carbon dots, these findings suggest that carbon dots may also gain access to the brain via amino acid transporters. However, when the transporters are depleted of amino acids, it becomes challenging for carbon dots to cross the BBB. To further test this hypothesis, in vivo imaging was performed on mice administered L-type amino acid transport 1 (LAT1) inhibitors and TQ CDs. The fluorescence signals observed in the brain were significantly reduced following the simultaneous administration of the LAT1 inhibitor and TQ CDs, in comparison to the mice receiving TQ CDs by gavage alone (Fig. 5H). This indicates that TQ CDs can gain access to the brain via amino acid transporters. Overall, the potential mechanism of carbon dots crossing the BBB was investigated, and both modes of administration demonstrated the capacity of TQ CDs to traverse the BBB and the aggregation of TQ CDs within the brain. Similarly, the intravenous effect can be achieved by increasing the concentration of the TQ CDs administered via gavage. This represents a significant advancement in the field of nanomaterials for oral drug delivery strategies, offering a promising avenue for overcoming the BBB obstacle.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protective effect of TQ CDs against brain injury
ce:section.ce:para: Given the antioxidant capacity of TQ CDs and their demonstrated ability to aggregate in the brain, the protective capacity of TQ CDs against brain injury was evaluated. Consequently, the protective effect of TQ CDs on brain damage characterized by neuronal reduction caused by MPTP was assessed in vivo, and Fig. 6 A shows the experimental process. Fig. 6B depicts the methodology employed to assess the behavioral responses of the mice using the rotarod test and open field test. Fig. 6C shows the residence time of each group of mice on the rotarod. It can be observed that the mice in the MPTP group exhibited inflexible and uncoordinated limbs, rendering them unable to remain on the rotarod for an extended period. Conversely, the two TQ CDs treatment groups demonstrated a significant improvement in this symptom. Similarly, Fig. 6G illustrates the trajectories of each group of mice in the open field. The mice in the MPTP group exhibited a markedly reduced level of mobility, in stark contrast to the mice in the two TQ CDs treatment groups, which appeared to be highly active. The observed behavioral improvements demonstrate that both intravenous and oral gavage administration of TQ CDs effectively alleviate MPTP-induced motor deficits, including bradykinesia and impaired locomotor activity. The total distance traveled by the mice through the open field test was quantified using Smart v3.0 software (Fig. 6F). Additionally, the same software was employed to analyze the mean velocity of the mice and the proportion of time they spent stationary in the open field (Fig. 6D-E). In general, the MPTP group exhibited a notable progression of Parkinsonian symptoms, which were effectively mitigated by the administration of TQ CDs. Furthermore, the degree of neuronal damage was assessed by Nissl staining of the substantia nigra (SN) in each group of mice (Fig. 6H) [51]. In the MPTP group, a distinct reduction in the dimensions of the Nissl body was evident, exhibiting an atrophic morphology. Furthermore, a notable decline in the number of Nissl bodies was observed. Fig. 6I illustrates the statistics of the number of Nissl bodies by ImageJ. In contrast, the Nissl bodies observed in the PBS group were characterized by a high degree of organization and visibility. Also, the Nissl bodies observed in the intravenous TQ CDs group and the gavage TQ CDs group were found to be neatly arranged and clearly visible. The considerable number of Nissl bodies indicated that both modes of administration were effective in inhibiting MPTP-induced neuronal damage. Moreover, immunohistochemical analysis was performed to assess the neuroprotective efficacy of TQ CDs in mitigating dopaminergic neuronal injury [52]. As the rate-limiting enzyme in dopamine biosynthesis, tyrosine hydroxylase (TH) is intrinsically linked to Parkinson's disease pathophysiology. The decreased catalytic activity of TH leads to a consequential reduction in dopamine synthesis, thereby precipitating the manifestation of clinical symptoms associated with the disorder. Fig. 6J illustrates the results of TH staining of the SN and striatum (STR), respectively. The mean optical density of the SN and STR regions was determined based on the results of the TH staining (Fig. 6K). The findings indicated that TQ CDs facilitated the mitigation of the reduction in TH expression resulting from MPTP. In general, both modes of administration were found to confer significant protection against injury to the SN and STR.

The synthesis and characterization of antioxidant NIR TQ CDs have been successfully completed, and the ability of TQ CDs to scavenge ROS and free radicals has been demonstrated. TQ CDs possess excellent imaging capabilities and are capable of accumulating in the liver and brain, both when administered intravenously and by gavage. In order to confirm the feasibility of gavage administration and to ensure that the effect is approximately equivalent to that of intravenous administration, this study proposed a straightforward methodology for attaining the relationship between gavage concentration and intravenous concentration. The imaging effect and biological effects achieved after intravenous injection can also be achieved by increasing the concentration of TQ CDs administered by gavage. Additionally, the antioxidant activity and aggregation capacity of TQ CDs in the liver and brain were found to exert a protective effect against cadmium-induced liver injury and neuronal damage caused by MPTP. The imaging and biological effects of TQ CDs in the brain represent a significant advancement in the field of nanoparticle-based oral drug delivery strategies for overcoming the BBB obstacle. Furthermore, the dual protective effect of the liver and the brain exhibited by TQ CDs may contribute to the advancement of research in the field of hepatic encephalopathy, thereby expanding the scope of research into carbon dots.
